thesis: Royal Bank of Canada is the largest Canadian bank by market cap and one of the world's top banks by assets. It delivers strong double-digit revenue and earnings growth, a 16% ROE, and a 2.4% dividend yield with 48 consecutive years of increases. At $210, the stock trades at a premium valuation (19.5x P/E, 3.2x P/B) and above most analyst targets after a 62% one-year rally. High-quality defensive compounder, but best bought on dips.

drivers: Rate-cut cycle stimulates mortgage originations and credit growth without a spike in delinquencies
Wealth management AUA grows 8-10% annually, adding ~$14B per 100 bps market appreciation
Capital Markets M&A and energy advisory rebound drives fee income
Investors re-rate the stock to 20-21x forward EPS on sustained double-digit earnings growth

base_case.assumptions: Moderate GDP growth supports mid-single-digit loan growth. NIM compresses 5-10 bps as deposit competition intensifies, but fee-based revenue offsets. Forward P/E stabilizes at 17-18x. Stock trades sideways as earnings catch up to valuation.

risks: Hard landing in Canada or U.S. triggers spike in loan-loss provisions (PCL > 60 bps)
Commercial real estate exposure deteriorates faster than modeled in Canadian and U.S. office markets
NIM compresses sharply as rates fall and deposit betas lag
Multiple contracts to 14-15x forward earnings, consistent with recessionary bank pricing

catalysts.event: Bank of Canada rate cuts continue, stimulating mortgage refinancing activity

key_risks: Canadian household debt-to-income is among the highest in the G7; unemployment spike could drive mortgage delinquencies
Commercial real estate exposure in Canadian and U.S. office markets remains elevated
Net interest margin compression as deposit competition intensifies and asset yields reprice faster
Capital Markets revenue cyclicality tied to M&A and trading volatility
Regulatory capital requirements (OSFI / Basel III) could limit buyback capacity or dividend growth
Canada-U.S. trade tensions and tariffs could weigh on cross-border capital flows and GDP growth
